serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side dish serving of stir-fried bean paste long beans is approximately 120 g, equivalent to a small bowl or side plate portion as commonly served in a Singaporean hawker or home meal.

nutrient_source.title: Stir Fried Long Beans with Bean Paste — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) entry for stir-fried long beans with bean paste (cr_id F14080032). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken verbatim from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on cooked green beans / legume vegetables (GI ~20)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried bean paste long beans. GI estimated at 20 (low) based on the closest analogous food: cooked green beans / yard-long beans, which are legume vegetables with high dietary fibre (4 g/100 g) and low net carbohydrate content. Reference: University of Sydney GI Database entry for green beans / legume vegetables (GI ~20). Glycemic load per 120 g serving = (20 × 6.5 × 1.20) / 100 = 1.56, rounded to 1.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 506 mg per 100 g — substantially above the 400 mg threshold, driven by soybean paste (tau cheo) and any added soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 60–80 g and request preparation with reduced bean paste and no added soy sauce to keep per-meal sodium below 200 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low net carbohydrate (6.5 g/100 g) with high dietary fibre (4 g/100 g) producing a low estimated glycaemic index (~20) and glycaemic load (~1.6 per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes at a standard 120 g serving; the high fibre content slows glucose absorption, but individuals should verify that bean paste used in preparation contains minimal added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (506 mg/100 g) is the primary cardiovascular concern; fat content is moderate (3.8 g/100 g) with low saturated fat (~0.6 g/100 g) and the dish is cholesterol-free
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat and high fibre profile are cardioprotective, but the high sodium content requires portion control and reduced-paste preparation for individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 506 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg RA threshold; chili is a nightshade ingredient that may trigger joint inflammation in susceptible individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request preparation without chili and with minimal bean paste to reduce sodium and nightshade solanine exposure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Bean Paste Long Beans (豆酱炒长豆, Kacang Panjang Goreng Pes Kacang) is a popular Singaporean vegetable side dish consisting of yard-long beans (snake beans) stir-fried at high heat with soybean paste (tau cheo or doubanjiang), garlic, chili, and a small amount of cooking oil. The dish is characterised by a savoury, slightly salty, and mildly spicy flavour profile with a tender-crisp texture. It is commonly served as a side dish in Singaporean hawker centres, coffee shops (kopitiams), and home kitchens, typically accompanying steamed rice. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 76 kcal, 3.9 g protein, 3.8 g fat, 6.5 g carbohydrate, and an exceptionally high dietary fibre content of 4.0 g, making it a high-fibre, low-calorie vegetable dish. The primary clinical concern is its sodium content of 506 mg/100 g, which is elevated due to the fermented soybean paste and any added soy sauce; this makes it unsuitable for hypertension and rheumatoid arthritis without portion control or ingredient modification. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the dish is well-suited due to its low glycaemic index (~20), high fibre, and moderate carbohydrate content. The dish is naturally vegetarian, vegan, and dairy-free, and contains soy as the primary allergen. It is a staple of Singaporean Chinese home cooking and is widely available across the island.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) at confidence 0.95 for an exact match to this dish (cr_id F14080032). All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are taken verbatim from this approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked legume vegetables.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, full vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific stir-fried preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) entry for F14080032 as of 2026-04-16. Potassium estimated at 220 mg/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central data for cooked yard-long beans (closest approved-source analogue); this is an approximation and should be verified against direct analysis. Saturated fat estimated at 0.6 g/100 g based on typical cooking oil contribution at the reported total fat level of 3.8 g/100 g for a stir-fried vegetable dish; this is an approximation. Sugar estimated at 2.5 g/100 g consistent with the input record health suitability note. Cholesterol set to 0.0 g as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. GI of 20 is an analogous estimate based on cooked green beans / yard-long beans from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific preparation exists in the approved GI source database. The dish contains soy as an allergen via the fermented soybean paste (tau cheo). Chili is a nightshade ingredient; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor tolerance.
